diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Unilateral bleeding localises to a single anterior septal vessel (Kiesselbach's plexus).
Posterior bleeds are more often bilateral or track heavily into the pharynx from the outset.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset immediately after vigorous nose-blowing on a background of allergic rhinitis and dry heated air

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mechanical shear on friable, dried allergic mucosa is the classic precipitant of an anterior Kiesselbach bleed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bleeding arrests with firm sustained pressure to the cartilaginous (soft) part of the nose

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Immediate control with correct anterior compression essentially confirms an anterior source.
A posterior bleed is not controlled by anterior pinch pressure; control by it excludes a significant posterior source.

symptoms.symptom_name: No active blood flow down the posterior pharyngeal wall during anterior compression

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of posterior pharyngeal flow while anterior pressure is applied argues strongly against a posterior source (clinician-observed in the source encounter; sealed).

symptoms.symptom_name: No anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, no personal or family bleeding history

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of anticoagulation and a negative bleeding history make a systemic coagulopathy unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No petechiae, purpura or bruising elsewhere on the skin

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of cutaneous bleeding stigmata further reduces the likelihood of a coagulopathy (clinician-observed; not obtainable over chat).

symptoms.symptom_name: Excoriated, actively oozing bleeding point at the anterior nasal septum (Kiesselbach's plexus)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Direct visualisation of the anterior septal bleeding point is diagnostic — obtainable only in person (sealed).

symptoms.symptom_name: Swallowed blood with resulting nausea (blood tracking posteriorly due to head-back posture)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A consequence of the incorrect head-back technique rather than a discriminator of source; flags that first aid needs correcting.

symptoms.symptom_name: Mild tachycardia (HR ~102) and mildly elevated BP (~145/90) with normal SpO2 (99%)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clinician-measured vitals consistent with acute distress/anxiety rather than haemodynamic compromise; sealed as these cannot be reliably obtained over chat here.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Unilateral bleeding + a clear mechanical/mucosal precipitant + control on correct anterior pressure together constitute a high-confidence benign anterior epistaxis that is telehealth-manageable with coaching.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count and coagulation profile (INR/APTT)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated for a first, controllable anterior bleed with no anticoagulant use and no bleeding history. Reserve for recurrent, atypical or difficult-to-control epistaxis. Routine ordering here is over-investigation.
Not required acutely; consider on routine referral if bleeds recur or are difficult to control, to identify and cauterise the bleeding point.
